stories.headline: NTSB Opens Inquiry Into Frontier Airlines Evacuation After Deadly Denver Runway Incident
stories.summary:
The National Transportation Safety Board said Sunday it is gathering information about the emergency evacuation of a Frontier Airlines jet after the plane struck and killed a person on a runway at Denver International Airport late Friday night. The plane, Flight 4345 bound for Los Angeles, was carrying 224 passengers and seven crew members when the pilot reported striking a pedestrian during takeoff at approximately 11:19 p.m. and aborted the takeoff due to a reported engine fire and smoke in the cabin.

Passengers were evacuated via emergency slides onto the tarmac; 12 received minor injuries and five were transported to hospitals. Passengers described being trapped in the smoke-filled cabin for several minutes before evacuation and then left in cold conditions on the runway. Video circulated showing some passengers descending slides with carry-on luggage.

NTSB spokesperson Sarah Taylor Sulick said the agency is determining whether the evacuation meets criteria for a full safety investigation. Denver International Airport CEO Phil Washington called the incident 'a horrible and preventable tragedy' resulting from an apparent trespasser who jumped a fence and crossed the runway. The identity of the deceased is pending confirmation by the Denver Office of the Medical Examiner.

stories.headline: Colorado Legislature Passes Scaled-Back AI Regulations Bill; Heads to Polis
stories.summary:
Colorado lawmakers have passed Senate Bill 189, a near-total rewrite of the state's 2024 artificial intelligence regulations, and sent it to Gov. Jared Polis for his signature. The bill passed both chambers with little resistance after being introduced May 1, with only eight Republican lawmakers voting against it across all floor votes.

The original 2024 law, which has not yet taken effect, was intended to protect residents from discriminatory AI decisions in employment, lending, and housing. The rewrite scales back those protections significantly, reducing the core requirement to a mandate that applicants be notified when AI plays a role in a consequential decision about their lives.

The legislation emerged from a closed-door task force convened by Polis, who had called for a moratorium on state-level AI regulations. The process involved two years of negotiations among tech companies, venture capitalists, hospitals, schools, and consumer protection groups, including a collapsed deal in August that was torpedoed by lobbying within half a day of its announcement. Sen. Robert Rodriguez, a Denver Democrat central to the negotiations, said the scale and impact of AI technology demands some regulatory response.

stories.headline: Denver Mayor Johnston Rejects DOJ Demand to Repeal City's Assault Weapons Ban
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston joined public safety and civic leaders in formally rejecting a demand from the U.S. Department of Justice that the city repeal its longstanding ban on assault weapons. The DOJ sent a letter to Denver last week indicating it would file a lawsuit if the city did not comply with the demand.

Johnston's rejection marks a direct confrontation between the city and federal authorities over local gun control policy. Denver has maintained its assault weapons ban as a matter of local law, and city officials are standing by the ordinance despite the federal pressure.

Separately, the DOJ filed a lawsuit against Colorado over one of the state's gun laws, adding to a pattern of federal legal challenges targeting Colorado firearms regulations. The dual actions reflect an escalating tension between Denver and Colorado on one side and the Trump administration on the other over gun policy.

stories.headline: Denver City Council to Consider Affordable Housing Preservation Ordinance Monday
stories.summary:
The Denver City Council will weigh an affordable housing preservation ordinance at its regular Monday meeting that would waive the city's rights of first refusal and first offer to acquire residential properties under a 2024 state law. The ordinance is designed to prevent older apartment buildings from being demolished and converted to luxury rentals by requiring that properties designated for long-term affordable housing remain affordable for at least 100 years.

Proponents argue the measure strengthens tenant protections and preserves existing affordable stock. Opponents contend it will slow new development needed to address Denver's housing shortage.

Also on Monday's agenda is a $715,000 contract amendment with the Colorado Nonprofit Development Center — the Harm Reduction Action Center — to purchase a permanent facility for drug education and needle exchange services, and a $225,000 liability settlement in a Denver Police Department civil case. The council will also take up a resolution that, if accepted, would trigger a public hearing on a proposed one-year moratorium on data centers in Denver.

stories.headline: Federal Lawsuit Alleges Trump Administration Withholds Colorado Wildfire Funds as Political Retaliation
stories.summary:
A Colorado Sun opinion column by Mike Littwin details the federal lawsuit brought by the nonprofit that manages the National Center for Atmospheric Research in Boulder, which argues the Trump administration is withholding funding from NCAR in retaliation for Colorado's refusal to pardon election denier Tina Peters and the state's use of mail-in voting. A federal judge heard the case Thursday, asking whether the two sides could resolve the dispute through negotiation.

Separate reporting from The Washington Post, cited in the column, found internal documents showing that 20 wildfire-related projects in the West lack federal funding, with the bulk concentrated in Colorado and California — both states whose governors have clashed publicly with President Trump. Colorado and California have received virtually no disaster funding from FEMA since Trump began boosting grants to other states last July.

The pattern reflects what critics describe as a politically motivated withdrawal of disaster preparedness resources from states that have defied the administration. Colorado faces significant wildfire risk and the loss of fire prevention funding has drawn concern from state officials.

stories.headline: Man Shot and Killed in South Thornton After Allegedly Breaking Into Vehicles
stories.summary:
A 34-year-old man was shot and killed in south Thornton early Sunday morning after he was allegedly breaking into vehicles in a residential neighborhood. Thornton police responded to the 9700 block of Ogden Court just after 3 a.m. following a 911 call reporting a vehicle break-in and shots fired.

Officers found the wounded man near a crashed truck, which investigators believe he was attempting to flee in after being shot but which collided with several parked cars. He was transported to a hospital, where he died. His identity will be released by the Adams County Coroner's Office.

A 23-year-old man was arrested on suspicion of murder but was released Sunday morning pending charges and further investigation. Police said the victim and suspect did not know each other. Anyone with information is asked to contact the Thornton Police Department tip line at 720-977-5069.

stories.headline: Denver Launches Small Business Loan Program Funded by Marijuana Tax Revenue
stories.summary:
The City and County of Denver has approved a small business loan program offering loans of up to $350,000 funded by marijuana sales tax revenue. The Denver City Council approved a Funding Assignment Agreement between Denver Economic Development and Opportunity and Colorado Enterprise Fund, with applications set to open June 1.

The program is designed to provide capital access for small businesses that may face barriers to traditional financing. Details on eligibility requirements and application specifics are expected to be released ahead of the June 1 opening date.

The use of marijuana tax revenue for economic development purposes reflects a continued effort by Denver to direct cannabis-generated funds toward community investment. The Colorado Enterprise Fund, a nonprofit lender, will administer the program.

stories.headline: Rockies Sit 16-25 at Season's Quarter Mark, On Pace for 20-Game Improvement Over 2025
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies stand 16-25 after one-quarter of the 2026 season, putting them on pace for a 63-99 record — a projected 20-game improvement over their franchise-worst 119-loss campaign in 2025. The team dropped a 6-0 decision to the Philadelphia Phillies on Sunday, with Kyle Schwarber hitting two solo home runs off Tomoyuki Sugano and Phillies ace Cristopher Sanchez dominating for seven innings.

Right-hander Chase Dollander has emerged as a bright spot, posting a 3.35 ERA compared to 6.98 as a rookie last season, though he continues to battle command issues. The team's road performance has also improved markedly: Colorado is 8-14 away from Coors Field but 6-4 over its last 10 road games, averaging 3.95 runs per contest compared to 2.81 per game last season.

The Rockies' four starting pitchers beyond Dollander are all veterans in the final year of their contracts, making them potential trade candidates at the August 3 deadline. Denver Post analysis notes the team is still last in the majors in starters' ERA at 5.27, but that figure represents a significant improvement over 2025's 6.65 mark.

stories.headline: Colorado Buffaloes Women's Lacrosse Upsets Denver in NCAA Tournament, Reaches First Quarterfinal Since 2002
stories.summary:
Eighth-seeded Colorado defeated rival Denver 11-9 in the NCAA Tournament second round Sunday at Prentup Field, earning the program's first NCAA quarterfinal berth in its 13-season history. The Buffaloes, now 16-3, tied the program record for wins and extended their winning streak to nine games.

Colorado trailed 5-3 early in the second quarter before scoring four consecutive goals to take a 7-5 halftime lead. Rachel Kennedy and Teagan Ryan each scored three goals, and goalie Elena Oh made seven saves as the Buffs held every opponent to single digits in goals for the entire season.

Denver's Olivia Ripple scored three goals to set a Pioneers season record with 73 on the year, but was held scoreless after the midpoint of the second half. Colorado will travel to face top-seeded Northwestern on Thursday. The quarterfinal appearance is the first for any CU team since the women's basketball squad reached the Elite Eight in 2002.

stories.headline: Rapids Fall 1-0 to St. Louis CITY, Drop to 10th in MLS Western Conference
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rapids lost 1-0 to St. Louis CITY at Dick's Sporting Goods Park on Saturday night, falling to 10th place in the MLS Western Conference and out of playoff position. It was the first home loss of coach Matt Wells's tenure and Colorado's second consecutive scoreless performance in league play.

The only goal came in the 26th minute on a long ball that found St. Louis forward Simon Becher, who flicked it to Jeong Sang-Bin for a one-on-one finish past backup goalkeeper Nico Hansen, who was starting in place of injured Zack Steffen. Defender Rob Holding was sent off with a straight red card in the 51st minute for denying an obvious scoring opportunity, a decision that stood after VAR review.

Despite playing down a man for nearly 40 minutes, the Rapids generated most of their expected goals in the second half, with the best chance coming on a header by Georgi Minoungou that sailed over the bar. Colorado has not won a league match since a 6-2 victory over Houston on April 11.

stories.headline: Colorado Ski Season Ends With 13 Fatalities at 11 Resorts, All Victims Were Men Ages 25-74
stories.summary:
The 2025-26 Colorado ski season closed with 13 fatalities across 11 resorts, according to data compiled by the Colorado Sun. All 13 victims were men ranging in age from 25 to 74. The causes of death included three heart attacks, nine deaths resulting from trauma, and one skier who suffocated after becoming trapped in a tree well.

Tree well deaths occur when a skier falls headfirst into the deep, loose snow surrounding the base of a tree and is unable to self-rescue. Colorado ski safety advocates have increasingly warned about the danger of tree wells, particularly in deep-snow seasons.

The final fatality count gives Colorado officials and resort operators data to assess safety protocols ahead of next season. The state is home to more than two dozen ski areas and draws millions of skiers and snowboarders annually.

stories.headline: Denver City Council Approves $20 Million for Westside Family Health Center; Property Tax Relief Applications Open
stories.summary:
The Denver City Council unanimously approved a $20 million funding agreement between the city and Denver Health for the Sam Sandos Westside Family Health Center, with the funds drawn from the voter-approved Vibrant Denver Bond. The health center will serve residents on Denver's west side.

Separately, the City and County of Denver announced it is now accepting applications for its 2025 Property Tax Relief Program, which provides partial refunds of property taxes paid — or the equivalent in rent — to eligible older adults, people with disabilities, low-income families, and qualifying surviving spouses.

The dual announcements come as Denver continues to navigate budget pressures and address housing affordability and health access for lower-income residents. Residents interested in the property tax relief program can apply through the city's official website.

weather.notable: About 16 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I'm a pretty normal guy. I just happen to eat a lot. Eating is my cardio.

topStory.headline: Hegseth Orders Second Pentagon Review of Sen. Kelly Over Weapons Stockpile Comments
topStory.summary:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth on Sunday directed Pentagon legal counsel to investigate Sen. Mark Kelly for a second time, accusing the Arizona Democrat of disclosing classified information during a television interview. Hegseth made the announcement via social media after Kelly appeared on CBS' "Face the Nation" and expressed concern about the depth of US weapons depletion during the ongoing war with Iran.

Kelly, a retired Navy captain and former astronaut who serves on the Senate Armed Services and Intelligence committees, told CBS anchor Margaret Brennan that Pentagon briefings had revealed alarming drawdowns of munitions including Tomahawks, ATACMS, and Patriot rounds. "We've expended a lot of munitions. And that means the American people are less safe," Kelly said, warning that stockpile depletion created vulnerabilities in potential future conflicts, including in the western Pacific. Kelly fired back at Hegseth, posting a video of the two at a recent Senate hearing in which Hegseth himself stated it would take "years" to replenish certain stockpiles, arguing the information was already on the public record.

The confrontation is the second such clash between Hegseth and Kelly. In January, Hegseth announced administrative action against Kelly — including efforts to reduce the senator's retired military rank and issue a letter of censure — after Kelly and five other Democrats with military or intelligence backgrounds posted a November video calling on US service members to refuse illegal orders. Kelly sued Hegseth over those actions, and a federal judge ruled them unconstitutionally retaliatory. Last week, a majority of a three-judge panel at the DC US Circuit Court of Appeals appeared ready to reject the Justice Department's effort to revive the punitive measures.

The dispute unfolds against a backdrop of broader concern over US military readiness. CNN previously reported that the US military has significantly drawn down key missile stockpiles during the Iran conflict, creating what experts and people familiar with internal Defense Department assessments described as a "near-term risk" of ammunition shortfalls should another conflict arise within the next few years. The latest investigation call raises fresh questions about the boundaries between classified information and publicly stated government positions, as well as the administration's use of administrative tools against sitting legislators.
